startScreenOptions.title: AN64.1-AN64.3 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN64.1-AN64.3.

interactions.label: What is the key structural difference that explains why myelomeningocele causes

params.question: What is the key structural difference that explains why myelomeningocele causes paralysis but meningocele does not?

answers.text: Myelomeningocele occurs only in the cervical spine, affecting upper motor neurons

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Myelomeningocele most commonly occurs in the lumbosacral region. The paralysis results from herniation of the spinal cord through the vertebral defect, not the spinal level.

answers.text: In myelomeningocele, the spinal cord and nerve roots herniate through the defect; in meningocele, only the meninges herniate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: In myelomeningocele the malformed spinal cord herniates along with the meninges, directly damaging neural tissue and causing motor and sensory deficits. In meningocele the cord remains within the vertebral canal, preserving neurological function.

answers.text: Meningocele is always covered by thick bone, providing structural protection to the cord

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The vertebral arch is deficient in both conditions. The critical distinction is whether the spinal cord itself is displaced into the herniating sac, not the presence of a bony covering.

answers.text: In meningocele, neural tissue is exposed; in myelomeningocele, the sac is skin-covered

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This reverses the actual distinction. In myelomeningocele, neural tissue is visibly exposed through a thin membrane. In meningocele, the sac is covered by normal skin and contains no neural tissue.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key difference is what herniates. In myelomeningocele, the spinal cord and nerve roots protrude through the vertebral defect, causing paralysis and loss of bladder control. In meningocele, only the meninges and CSF herniate — the cord stays in the canal and function is preserved.

Exactly right. The presence of spinal cord tissue within the herniated sac is what determines neurological outcome — cord herniation in myelomeningocele causes irreversible damage, whereas meningocele spares the cord entirely.

interactions.label: Why does the primary motor cortex appear 'agranular' on histological examination

params.question: Why does the primary motor cortex appear 'agranular' on histological examination?

answers.text: Its layer V (internal pyramidal layer) is absent, leaving no large neurons

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Layer V is actually expanded in the motor cortex, not absent. It contains Betz cells — the largest neurons in the CNS — which form the corticospinal tract.

answers.text: It contains no neuronal cell bodies, only myelinated axons

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: All cortical areas contain neuronal cell bodies organised across their layers. The term 'agranular' refers to the reduction of one specific layer, not the absence of neurons.

answers.text: Its layer IV (internal granular layer) is very thin or absent

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The primary motor cortex is classified as agranular because its internal granular layer (layer IV) — normally the main thalamic sensory input layer — is markedly reduced. Instead, layer V is prominently expanded with large Betz cells that project via the corticospinal tract.

answers.text: Its molecular layer (layer I) is replaced by white matter tracts

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The molecular layer is present in all cortical areas and consists mainly of neuropil. The term 'agranular' specifically refers to the near-absence of the internal granular layer (layer IV).

overallFeedback.feedback: The primary motor cortex is called 'agranular' because its layer IV (internal granular layer) is very thin or absent. This contrasts with the primary sensory cortex (koniocortex), where layer IV is thick and prominent for receiving thalamic input.
Well done. The motor cortex is an output-dominant area, so its sensory input layer (IV) is reduced while its output layer (V) is expanded — the defining feature of agranular cortex.

metadata.title: Why does the primary motor cortex appear

interactions.label: A prenatal ultrasound at 18 weeks reveals absence of the cranial vault with expo

params.question: A prenatal ultrasound at 18 weeks reveals absence of the cranial vault with exposed, degenerating neural tissue. This condition most likely resulted from failure of:

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is anencephaly — the most severe cranial neural tube defect. It results from failure of the cranial neuropore to close by day 25. Exposed neural tissue degenerates in amniotic fluid, making this condition incompatible with life.
Failure of the caudal neuropore to close by day 28 causes spinal defects (spina bifida spectrum), not cranial vault abnormalities. Cranial defects arise from the cranial neuropore.

Neural crest cells give rise to structures such as dorsal root ganglia, Schwann cells, and the adrenal medulla. Their migration failure does not cause absence of the cranial vault or cerebral hemispheres.

answers.text: Cortical neuron migration along radial glial fibres

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Cortical neuron migration determines the inside-out layering of the cerebral cortex. It occurs after the neural tube has already closed and does not affect vault formation.

overallFeedback.feedback: The cranial vault and cerebral hemispheres fail to develop when the cranial neuropore does not close by day 25 — this is anencephaly. Spinal defects (myelomeningocele, meningocele, spina bifida occulta) result from caudal neuropore failure by day 28.

Correct. Anencephaly is the most devastating cranial NTD, caused by cranial neuropore closure failure by day 25. This is largely preventable with 400 micrograms of folic acid daily from one month before conception.
